
### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Implant Material Selection Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Choose Maxillofacial Implant Material] --> Q1{Patient Age}
    Q1 -->|Pediatric Growing| R1[Resorbable PLA or PLLA]
    Q1 -->|Adult| Q2{Load Bearing}
    Q2 -->|Yes Load Bearing| Q3{Young Modulus Match Needed}
    Q2 -->|No Esthetic Only| R2[Medpor or Silicone]

    Q3 -->|Yes Reduce Stress Shielding| R3[PEEK Patient Specific Implant]
    Q3 -->|No Standard| R4[CP Titanium Grade 4]

    Q2 -->|Defect Reconstruction Custom| R5[3D Printed Ti Alloy PSI]

    R1 --> End[Final Implant Selection]
    R2 --> End
    R3 --> End
    R4 --> End
    R5 --> End

    style Start fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style End fill:#065f46,color:#ffffff
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Set]**
**Define Maxillofacial Surgery. List any four common procedures performed under this specialty.**

**Model Answer:**
Maxillofacial surgery is the surgical specialty dealing with the diagnosis and treatment of diseases, injuries, and defects of the face, jaws, oral cavity, and associated structures.
Four common procedures:
1. Open reduction and internal fixation (ORIF) of mandibular fractures.
2. Orthognathic surgery (Le Fort I osteotomy, BSSO).
3. Cleft lip and palate repair.
4. Total temporomandibular joint (TMJ) replacement.

*(CO Mapping: CO4 | Bloom Level: Remember | 3 marks)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Set]**
**What is distraction osteogenesis? State the standard distraction rate and consolidation period.**

**Model Answer:**
Distraction osteogenesis is a biological process in which new bone is generated between two bone segments that are gradually separated by controlled mechanical traction, following a surgical osteotomy.
- **Latency phase:** 5–7 days.
- **Distraction rate:** **1 mm/day** (typically in 0.5 mm twice-daily increments).
- **Consolidation period:** **~2 months per cm** of distraction.

*(CO Mapping: CO4 | Bloom Level: Understand | 3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme Pattern]**
**(a)** Classify the various types of fixation systems used in maxillofacial fracture management. Describe the **biomechanical principle of locking plates** with suitable diagrams. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** A 30-year-old patient has a comminuted mandibular body fracture. The surgeon plans to use a 2.0 mm locking reconstruction plate. If the maximum bending moment at the fracture site is **15 N·m**, plate width is **5 mm**, and plate thickness is **2.5 mm**, calculate the maximum bending stress and comment on whether CP-Titanium (yield strength ≈ 550 MPa) is safe. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification of Fixation Systems:**

> [!NOTE] **Classification Outline (Full marks require headings + 1–2 line explanation each)**
> 1. **Non-locking (compression) plates** – require plate-to-bone contact; screw torque compresses plate onto bone.
> 2. **Locking plates** – screw head locks into plate threads; creates fixed-angle construct; no need for plate-bone contact → preserves periosteal blood supply.
> 3. **Miniplates (2.0 mm)** – Champy's principle, monocortical screws, ideal for mandible.
> 4. **Microplates (1.0–1.5 mm)** – thin bones (orbital rim, NOE complex).
> 5. **Reconstruction plates (2.0–2.5 mm)** – load-bearing, comminuted fractures, defects.
> 6. **Resorbable plates (PLA/PLLA)** – pediatric, non-load-bearing.
> 7. **Patient-Specific 3D-printed plates** – complex defects, oncology.

**Biomechanical Principle of Locking Plates (Diagram Description):**
In a locking plate, the **threaded screw head engages with mating threads in the plate hole**, creating a **single rigid construct**. Load is transferred from bone → screw → plate → screw → bone, **bypassing the plate-bone interface**. This eliminates the need for plate-bone friction, hence preserves periosteal vascularity and reduces stress shielding.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1:** Given $M = 15$ N·m, $b = 5$ mm $= 5 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $h = 2.5$ mm $= 2.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m.

**Step 2:** Second moment of area:

$$
I = \frac{b h^{3}}{12} = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-3})(2.5 \times 10^{-3})^{3}}{12} = 6.51 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m}^{4}
$$

**Step 3:** Distance from neutral axis:

$$
c = \frac{h}{2} = 1.25 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4:** Bending stress:

$$
\sigma_{b} = \frac{M c}{I} = \frac{15 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-3}}{6.51 \times 10^{-12}} = 2.88 \times 10^{9} \text{ Pa} = 2.88 \text{ GPa}
$$

**Step 5:** Compare with yield strength:

$$
\sigma_{b} = 2880 \text{ MPa} \quad \gg \quad \sigma_{y} = 550 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Conclusion:** The computed bending stress **far exceeds** the yield strength of CP-Titanium. The plate as dimensioned would **fail by plastic deformation**. The surgeon must either (i) use a **thicker plate** (≥ 3.0 mm), (ii) add a **load-sharing bone graft**, or (iii) use a **higher-strength Ti-6Al-4V alloy** plate. *(Full marks require interpretation + clinical recommendation.)*

*(CO Mapping: CO4 | Bloom Level: Apply / Analyze | 14 marks)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [ALTERNATIVE CHOICE]**
**[KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme Pattern]**
**(a)** Explain in detail the **principle, phases, and clinical applications of distraction osteogenesis** in maxillofacial surgery. Mention the Ilizarov strain rate criteria. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Discuss the **role of biomaterials** in maxillofacial implants. Compare CP-Titanium, Ti-6Al-4V, PEEK, and resorbable polymers with respect to their mechanical properties, biocompatibility, and clinical indications. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Distraction Osteogenesis — Full Explanation:**

**Definition:** Biological process of new bone generation under controlled mechanical distraction of a surgically created bone segment.

**Three Phases (with timing):**

| Phase | Duration | Biological Event |
|---|---|---|
| **Latency** | 5–7 days | Callus formation, inflammatory phase |
| **Activation** | 1 mm/day | Neohistogenesis in distraction gap |
| **Consolidation** | ~2 months / cm | Mineralization, corticalization |

**Ilizarov Strain Rate Criterion:** Optimal regenerate forms when strain rate lies between **$10^{-7}$ and $10^{-6}$ s$^{-1}$**. Beyond this range → either premature consolidation (too fast) or fibrous non-union (too slow).

**Clinical Applications in Maxillofacial Region:**
1. Mandibular lengthening in hemifacial microsomia / Pierre Robin sequence.
2. Maxillary advancement in cleft patients (RED device).
3. Alveolar ridge distraction for implant site preparation.
4. Transport distraction for segmental defects.
5. Cranial vault expansion in craniosynostosis.

**(b) Biomaterials Comparison:**

| Property | CP-Ti (Grade 4) | Ti-6Al-4V | PEEK | Resorbable (PLLA) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Young's Modulus | 110 GPa | 114 GPa | 3–4 GPa | 2–4 GPa |
| Yield Strength | 550 MPa | 880 MPa | 90–100 MPa | 50–70 MPa |
| Biocompatibility | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent | Good (acidic degradation) |
| Radiopacity | Good | Good | Poor (radiolucent) | Poor |
| Clinical Use | Mini/microplates, screws | Reconstruction plates, PSI | Cranioplasty, PSI | Pediatric fractures |

**Key Discussion Points:**
- **CP-Ti** is the gold standard for maxillofacial plates; excellent biocompatibility due to passive oxide layer.
- **Ti-6Al-4V** offers higher strength; used in load-bearing reconstruction plates and 3D-printed PSI.
- **PEEK** is modulus-matched closer to bone (3–4 GPa vs. cortical bone 15 GPa) → **reduces stress shielding**; radiolucent → does not interfere with imaging/radiotherapy.
- **Resorbable polymers** (PLLA, PGA) eliminate the need for removal surgery in pediatric patients; **drawback** — bulkiness, lower strength, and acidic degradation byproducts.

*(CO Mapping: CO4 | Bloom Level: Understand / Analyze | 14 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing locking vs non-locking biomechanics** — examiners specifically want the "**fixed-angle construct + periosteal blood supply preservation**" points for full credit.
> 2. **Skipping the consolidation formula (2 months/cm)** — this is a favourite one-liner KTU asks every cycle.
> 3. **Numerical error on bending stress** — most students forget that $c = h/2$ (not $h$); this halves your stress and changes the answer.
> 4. **Not mentioning Ilizarov strain rate window** — direct **2-mark deduction** if omitted.
> 5. **Forgetting units (Pa vs MPa)** in numerical answers — **1 mark** lost per missing unit.
> 6. **Biomaterial comparison without mentioning Young's modulus mismatch and stress shielding** — partial credit only.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024**

- ✅ Maxillofacial surgery = diagnosis + surgical Rx of face, jaws, oral cavity, neck, and cranio-facial skeleton.
- ✅ Key subspecialties: trauma, orthognathic, oncology + reconstruction, cleft + craniofacial, TMJ, distraction, esthetic.
- ✅ **Le Fort classification** (I/II/III) — high-yield for diagrams.
- ✅ **Champy's principle** — ideal osteosynthesis line for mandible.
- ✅ **Locking plate = fixed-angle construct**, preserves periosteal blood supply.
- ✅ **Distraction osteogenesis rate = 1 mm/day**, latency 5–7 days, consolidation 2 months/cm.
- ✅ **Ilizarov strain rate window: 10⁻⁷ to 10⁻⁶ s⁻¹**.
- ✅ **Bending stress on plate:** $\sigma_b = M c / I$, with $I = b h^3 / 12$.
- ✅ **Bite force on molars ≈ 70–700 N** — used for implant load design.
- ✅ **CP-Ti Grade 4:** $E = 110$ GPa, $\sigma_y = 550$ MPa — gold standard for maxillofacial miniplates.
- ✅ **PEEK:** $E = 3–4$ GPa — modulus-matched implant, radiolucent, ideal for PSI/cranioplasty.
- ✅ **Resorbables (PLLA/PGA):** pediatric fractures, avoids second surgery; drawback = acidic byproducts + lower strength.
- ✅ **Patient-Specific 3D-printed implants (PSI):** reduces OR time by ~30–40%, essential for complex defects.
- ✅ **TMJ prosthesis:** typically Co-Cr condyle + UHMWPE fossa component (familiarise for KTU viva).
- ✅ **TMJ articulation:** ginglymoarthrodial joint (translation + rotation) — unique biomechanics.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Upper Limb Prosthetics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBMT724 - ARTIFICIAL ORGANS AND IMPLANTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Upper Limb Prosthetics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Upper Limb Prosthetics

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Upper Limb Prosthetics** is the specialized branch of biomedical engineering and rehabilitation science concerned with the design, fabrication, fitting, and functional restoration of artificial devices (prostheses) that substitute for the anatomical, biomechanical, and sometimes cosmetic functions of a missing or congenitally absent portion of the human upper extremity — from the finger tips up to the shoulder girdle.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 4):**
> *Upper limb prosthetics deals with the replacement of the entire or partial loss of the hand, forearm, arm, or shoulder using mechanical, electrical, or hybrid systems, with the objective of restoring prehension, manipulation, and cosmetic appearance to the amputee.*

The field is governed by a unique interdisciplinary convergence of:
- **Mechanical Engineering** (linkages, levers, cable systems)
- **Electrical & Electronic Engineering** (myoelectric sensors, microcontrollers, motors)
- **Materials Science** (carbon fiber, thermoplastics, silicone, titanium)
- **Anatomy & Physiology** (residual limb biomechanics, muscle physiology)
- **Rehabilitation Medicine** (training, occupational therapy)

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Custom Multi-Tool for a Missing Hand"

Imagine you have lost your dominant hand in an accident. You still have a powerful shoulder and a short remnant of the forearm. You need a device that:
1. Can **grasp** a coffee cup,
2. Can **lift** a heavy bag,
3. Can **type** on a keyboard,
4. Looks **reasonably normal** in public.

An upper-limb prosthesis is essentially a **personalized multi-tool** that the amputee wears strapped to the residual limb. The **socket** is the "custom handle" molded exactly to your stump, the **cable or myoelectric sensor** is the "instruction wire" telling the device what to do, and the **terminal device** (hook/hand) is the "business end" that actually grips. The key engineering challenge is that the device must be light (often < 500 g for a hand unit) yet strong enough to lift **5–10 kg** in a work setting.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU High-Yield Glossary)

| Term | Engineering Definition |
|---|---|
| **Residual Limb** (Stump) | The remaining portion of the amputated limb, distal to the amputation plane. |
| **Socket** | The custom-molded interface that couples the residual limb to the prosthesis. |
| **Suspension System** | The mechanism (suction, pin-lock, harness) that holds the prosthesis onto the residual limb. |
| **Terminal Device (TD)** | The end-effector that replaces the hand — either a hook, cosmetic glove, or functional hand. |
| **Control Cable** | A stainless-steel or spectra-cable that transmits body movement to operate body-powered devices. |
| **Myoelectric Signal (EMG)** | Electrical activity of residual muscles (typically **20–200 µV**, frequency **20–500 Hz**) used to control externally powered devices. |
| **Prehension** | The act of grasping or gripping — the primary functional goal of any upper-limb prosthesis. |
| **Cosmesis** | The cosmetic/appearance quality of the prosthesis. |

## 1.4 Levels of Upper-Limb Amputation (KTU Standard Classification)

The prosthesis design depends critically on the **level of amputation**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Standard Amputation Levels (from distal to proximal):**
> 1. **Partial Hand (Transcarpal)** — loss within the hand, wrist preserved.
> 2. **Wrist Disarticulation** — separation at the wrist joint.
> 3. **Transradial (Below-Elbow, BE)** — amputation between elbow and wrist. Most common level (~60% of cases).
> 4. **Elbow Disarticulation** — separation at the elbow.
> 5. **Transhumeral (Above-Elbow, AE)** — amputation between shoulder and elbow.
> 6. **Shoulder Disarticulation** — loss at the glenohumeral joint.
> 7. **Forequarter (Interscapulothoracic)** — removal of the entire arm including the scapula and clavicle.

The general rule: **the more proximal the amputation, the more complex and expensive the prosthesis, and the lower the functional restoration achieved.**

## 1.5 Epidemiology & Clinical Relevance

- Approximately **1.6 million** people in India live with upper-limb amputation (estimated, 2023).
- Trauma (road/industrial accidents) accounts for **> 80%** of cases in developing countries.
- Congenital limb deficiency occurs in roughly **4 per 10,000 live births**.
- Male-to-female ratio is approximately **5:1**, reflecting occupational exposure patterns.
- Bilateral amputation represents only **3–5%** but is the most devastating functional loss.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Components of an Upper-Limb Prosthesis

A complete upper-limb prosthesis, regardless of its actuation philosophy, is composed of **five functional subsystems**:

### 2.1.1 The Socket (Interface Subsystem)
- **Function:** Transfers all loads (static weight, dynamic grip forces, cable tension) between the residual limb and the prosthesis.
- **Design Principle:** The socket must achieve **total surface bearing (TSB)** to avoid high-pressure points that cause skin breakdown.
- **Material:** Thermoplastics (polypropylene, polyethylene) for low-cost, laminated carbon-fiber/epoxy for high-performance devices.
- **Pressure Constraint:** Maximum safe interface pressure on skin is approximately **40 kPa (300 mmHg)** over bony prominences.

### 2.1.2 The Suspension System
- **Anatomical Suspension:** Uses bony contours (e.g., the medial epicondyle for below-elbow).
- **Suction Suspension:** Achieved by an air-tight seal and a one-way valve; requires a snug socket.
- **Pin Lock:** A metal pin on a silicone liner engages a locking mechanism in the socket.
- **Harness Suspension:** A figure-8 or shoulder saddle harness with a control cable.

### 2.1.3 The Control System
There are three control philosophies:

> [!NOTE]
> **A) Body-Powered (Mechanical) Systems**
> - Operated by gross body movements transmitted through a cable.
> - Force amplified by a factor of **2–5×** through Bowden cable mechanics.
> - **Pros:** Robust, low cost, high sensory feedback (cable tension is felt), waterproof.
> - **Cons:** Cosmetically poor, requires a harness, tiring for prolonged use.

> [!NOTE]
> **B) Externally Powered (Myoelectric) Systems**
> - Electrodes (typically **differential surface Ag/AgCl electrodes**) detect residual-muscle EMG.
> - Signals are rectified, smoothed (typically with a **50 Hz** notch filter for powerline noise), and thresholded.
> - Above a threshold of approximately **20 µV** (after amplification by **~1000×**), a DC motor drives the hand or elbow.
> - **Pros:** Better cosmesis, higher grip force (up to **100 N**), no harness.
> - **Cons:** Expensive, battery-dependent, slower response (~**300 ms** vs. **100 ms** body-powered).

> [!NOTE]
> **C) Hybrid Systems**
> - Combine body-powered elbow with myoelectric terminal device — common for transhumeral amputees.

### 2.1.4 The Actuation System
- **Voluntary Opening (VO):** The grip is closed by a spring; the user must actively open it. Always-on gripping. Standard for body-powered.
- **Voluntary Closing (VC):** The user must actively close the grip. Higher pinch force (**> 100 N**) but tiring. Often preferred for bilateral amputees.

### 2.1.5 The Terminal Device
- **Hook (Functional):** Two-fingered (Dorrance-style) devices, forceps-like. Best functional efficiency.
- **Cosmetic Glove:** PVC or silicone, restores appearance but has poor grip.
- **Functional Hand:** Myoelectric hands with 3-jaw chuck, key, and power grips. Examples: **Ottobock Michelangelo, Bebionic, i-limb**.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Sheet

| # | Concept / Parameter | Formula or Numerical Value | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pinch / Grip Force (body-powered) | $F_{grip} = F_{cable} \cdot M_{lever}$ | **N**; $M_{lever}$ = 2 to 5 |
| 2 | Cable Mechanical Advantage | $MA = \frac{D_{harness}}{D_{anchor}}$ | Dimensionless; typical **2:1 to 5:1** |
| 3 | Cable Operating Force | $F_{op} = F_{grip} / MA$ | Typically **20–50 N** sustained by shoulder |
| 4 | EMG Signal Amplitude (residual muscle) | $V_{EMG} \approx 20$ to $200$ | **µV** (RMS, surface electrode) |
| 5 | EMG Dominant Frequency Band | $f_{EMG} \approx 20$ to $500$ | **Hz**; usable band **80–250 Hz** |
| 6 | Notch Filter Frequency | $f_{notch} = 50$ (India) | **Hz** to remove powerline interference |
| 7 | Safe Skin Interface Pressure | $P_{skin} \le 40$ | **kPa** (over bony prominences) |
| 8 | Prosthesis Mass (BE, body-powered) | $m \approx 300$ to $500$ | **g** |
| 9 | Battery Voltage (myoelectric) | $V_{bat} = 7.4$ | **V** Li-ion (typical) |
| 10 | Response Time (myoelectric) | $t_{resp} \approx 250$ to $400$ | **ms** |
| 11 | Voluntary Opening Hook Force | $F_{VO} \approx 30$ to $70$ | **N** |
| 12 | Voluntary Closing Hook Force | $F_{VC} \approx 100$ to $200$ | **N** |
| 13 | Working Range (Prosthetic Elbow) | $\theta_{elbow} \approx 5° \text{ to } 135°$ | Degrees from full extension |
| 14 | Cosmesis Rating Scale | 0 (poor) – 4 (excellent) | Subjective but standardized in KTU papers |
| 15 | Suspension Slip Force | $F_{slip} \ge 1.5 \times W_{prosthesis}$ | Newtons; $W$ = weight of prosthesis |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Anchor:**
> - Body-powered = **2/3 the cost**, **2/3 the weight**, **2/3 the grip force** of myoelectric — but **2/3 the cosmetic score**.
> - Mnemonic: **"2-2-2 Rule"** for body-powered vs. myoelectric comparison.

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind the Design

The engineer must always balance the **Trilogy of Prosthetic Design**:

1. **Function** (prehension, range, force)
2. **Cosmesis** (appearance, social acceptance)
3. **Comfort** (weight, suspension, temperature, skin health)

No single prosthesis can simultaneously maximize all three; trade-offs are explicit and patient-specific. The KTU syllabus expects students to articulate these trade-offs clearly during design discussions.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Upper-limb prosthetics has directly driven advances in:
- **Robotic end-effectors** (parallel-jaw grippers, underactuated hands)
- **Haptic feedback systems** (vibrotactile, electrotactile)
- **Pattern-recognition EMG** (using **machine-learning classifiers** to identify intended movements from multi-channel EMG)
- **Osseointegration** (direct skeletal attachment via titanium implants, eliminating the socket)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cable Mechanical Advantage in a Body-Powered Transradial Prosthesis

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

A transradial amputee uses a body-powered prosthesis with a figure-8 harness attached to the contralateral (opposite) shoulder. The harness cable travels a horizontal distance $D_{harness}$ at the shoulder before anchoring to the prosthesis at distance $D_{anchor}$ from the elbow axis. Determine the mechanical advantage and the operating force required to achieve a 50 N grip on a terminal device.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define Mechanical Advantage (MA)**

The mechanical advantage of a cable-driven linkage is defined as the ratio of output force (grip force) to input force (shoulder force):

$$
MA \;=\; \frac{F_{out}}{F_{in}} \;=\; \frac{F_{grip}}{F_{shoulder}}
$$

**Step 2 — Geometric Relationship**

For a Bowden-cable system, the cable moves a distance proportional to the lever-arm length. From similar triangles in the geometry:

$$
MA \;=\; \frac{D_{harness}}{D_{anchor}}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Numerical Values**

Let $D_{harness} = 100 \text{ mm}$ and $D_{anchor} = 25 \text{ mm}$.

$$
MA \;=\; \frac{100 \text{ mm}}{25 \text{ mm}} \;=\; 4
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for Required Shoulder Force**

Required grip force $F_{grip} = 50 \text{ N}$.

$$
F_{shoulder} \;=\; \frac{F_{grip}}{MA} \;=\; \frac{50 \text{ N}}{4} \;=\; 12.5 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation**

A shoulder force of only **12.5 N** (roughly the force needed to lift a 1.25 kg mass) is sufficient to generate a 50 N grip. This is why body-powered devices are still preferred for heavy manual labor despite their cosmetic drawbacks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The trade-off is that the cable must move **4 times** the distance. A 10 mm gripper closure requires a **40 mm** shoulder excursion, which is why body-powered systems demand a generous range of motion.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Grip Force from a Myoelectric Hand

### 3.2.1 Problem Setup

A myoelectric prosthetic hand is driven by a **7.4 V DC motor** with an internal resistance of $R_m = 2.5 \, \Omega$. The motor drives a finger through a planetary gearbox of ratio $N = 200:1$ and a final finger lever-arm of $L = 30 \text{ mm}$. The motor draws a stall current of $I_{stall} = 2.96 \text{ A}$ at the rated voltage. Estimate the maximum finger-tip grip force (assume the motor is stalled).

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Stall Torque of the Motor**

The electrical input power at stall is $P_{stall} = V \cdot I_{stall}$.

$$
P_{stall} \;=\; 7.4 \, \text{V} \times 2.96 \, \text{A} \;=\; 21.9 \, \text{W}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert Electrical Power to Mechanical Stall Torque**

Assuming ~**70%** electromechanical efficiency at stall ($\eta = 0.70$):

$$
\tau_{stall} \;=\; \frac{\eta \cdot P_{stall}}{\omega_{no\,load}}
$$

We need the no-load speed. For a small DC motor of this class, a typical $\omega_{no\,load} \approx 10\,000 \text{ rpm} = 1047 \text{ rad/s}$.

$$
\tau_{stall} \;=\; \frac{0.70 \times 21.9}{1047} \;\approx\; 0.0146 \, \text{N·m} \;=\; 14.6 \, \text{mN·m}
$$

**Step 3 — Gearbox Output Torque**

$$
\tau_{finger} \;=\; N \times \tau_{stall} \times \eta_{gear}
$$

With gearbox efficiency $\eta_{gear} \approx 0.85$:

$$
\tau_{finger} \;=\; 200 \times 0.0146 \times 0.85 \;\approx\; 2.48 \, \text{N·m}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Finger-Tip Force**

The finger tip force is torque divided by lever arm:

$$
F_{tip} \;=\; \frac{\tau_{finger}}{L} \;=\; \frac{2.48 \, \text{N·m}}{0.030 \, \text{m}} \;\approx\; 82.7 \, \text{N}
$$

**Step 5 — Comparison with Body-Powered**

A well-designed myoelectric hand generates ~**80–100 N** of pinch force, comparable to or higher than body-powered **voluntary-opening** hooks. However, the **battery cost** is roughly **2×** the cost of a cable system.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Suspension Safety Factor

### 3.3.1 Problem Setup

A transhumeral (above-elbow) body-powered prosthesis weighs $W_p = 1.2 \text{ kg}$ (including the harness and cable). Design a suspension such that the prosthesis will not slip off the residual limb even under dynamic acceleration of $a = 2g$.

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Effective Dynamic Weight**

$$
W_{dyn} \;=\; m \cdot g \cdot (1 + a/g) \;=\; m \cdot (g + a)
$$

With $a = 2g$:

$$
W_{dyn} \;=\; 1.2 \times (9.81 + 2 \times 9.81) \;=\; 1.2 \times 29.43 \;\approx\; 35.3 \, \text{N}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Safety Factor**

KTU/ISO standard safety factor for prosthetic suspension: $SF = 1.5$.

$$
F_{suspension\,req} \;\ge\; SF \times W_{dyn} \;=\; 1.5 \times 35.3 \;\approx\; 53.0 \, \text{N}
$$

**Step 3 — Translate to Friction Coefficient for Suction Socket**

For a suction suspension, the holding force equals the product of normal force (atmospheric) and friction coefficient. Atmospheric pressure supplies a normal force $F_{atm} = P_{atm} \times A_{seal}$, where $A_{seal}$ is the seal area.

For $A_{seal} = 30 \text{ cm}^2 = 3.0 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^2$:

$$
F_{atm} \;=\; 101325 \times 3.0 \times 10^{-3} \;\approx\; 304 \, \text{N}
$$

Required friction coefficient $\mu$:

$$
\mu \;\ge\; \frac{F_{suspension\,req}}{F_{atm}} \;=\; \frac{53.0}{304} \;\approx\; 0.17
$$

**Step 4 — Engineering Interpretation**

A silicone liner on skin provides $\mu \approx 0.4$–$0.7$, so suction suspension is **safe** with a large margin. However, the skin-seal must be airtight; even a **1 mm** gap can halve the effective suction force.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Prosthetic Type by Amputation Level

| Amputation Level | Recommended Control | Typical Mass | Typical Cost (India, 2024) | Functional Outcome Score* |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Partial Hand | Cosmetic / silicone partial | 50–150 g | ₹ 5,000 – 30,000 | 3.5 / 4 |
| Wrist Disarticulation | Body-powered or myoelectric | 200–400 g | ₹ 30,000 – 2,50,000 | 3.0 / 4 |
| Transradial (long) | Body-powered | 300–500 g | ₹ 25,000 – 1,50,000 | 2.8 / 4 |
| Transradial (short) | Myoelectric preferred | 400–700 g | ₹ 1,50,000 – 6,00,000 | 2.5 / 4 |
| Elbow Disarticulation | Hybrid (cable elbow + myo TD) | 800–1200 g | ₹ 2,50,000 – 8,00,000 | 2.2 / 4 |
| Transhumeral | Hybrid (cable elbow + myo TD) | 1.0–1.5 kg | ₹ 3,00,000 – 10,00,000 | 1.8 / 4 |
| Shoulder Disarticulation | Myoelectric + body-powered | 1.5–2.5 kg | ₹ 5,00,000 – 15,00,000 | 1.2 / 4 |

*Functional Outcome Score is the standardized 0–4 scale used in KTU practical examinations (4 = full functional restoration).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Functional Subsystems of a Complete Upper-Limb Prosthesis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[RESIDUAL LIMB] --> B[SOCKET INTERFACE]
    B --> C[SUSPENSION SYSTEM]
    C --> D[CONTROL SUBSYSTEM]
    D --> E[ACTUATION SUBSYSTEM]
    E --> F[TERMINAL DEVICE]
    B -. Pressure .-> G[SKIN HEALTH MONITOR]
    D -. EMG Signal .-> H[POWER SUPPLY BATTERY]
    E -. Feedback .-> I[MYOELECTRIC CONTROLLER]
    H --> I
    I --> E
```

**Node Meanings (Left to Right):**
- RESIDUAL LIMB: biological remnant providing muscular and sensory input.
- SOCKET INTERFACE: load-transfer and sensory-coupling zone.
- SUSPENSION SYSTEM: holds the device onto the residual limb.
- CONTROL SUBSYSTEM: harness cable or myoelectric amplifier.
- ACTUATION SUBSYSTEM: motor, gearbox, cable, or hydraulic cylinder.
- TERMINAL DEVICE: hook, cosmetic hand, or myoelectric hand.
- SKIN HEALTH MONITOR: pressure/temperature sensors.
- POWER SUPPLY: Li-ion battery (typically 7.4 V, 1500 mAh).
- MYOELECTRIC CONTROLLER: micro-controller with EMG classifier.

## 4.2 Decision Tree — Selecting the Prosthesis Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[NEW AMPUTEE EVALUATION] --> A{Amputation Level}
    A -->|Transradial| B{Residual Length}
    A -->|Transhumeral| C{Residual Length}
    A -->|Shoulder| D[Body Powered Hybrid]
    B -->|Greater than 50 percent| E[Body Powered Hook]
    B -->|Less than 50 percent| F[Myoelectric Hand]
    C -->|Greater than 35 percent| G[Hybrid Cable Elbow plus Myo Hand]
    C -->|Less than 35 percent| H[Body Powered Elbow plus Myo Hand]
    E --> I{Cosmesis Priority}
    F --> I
    I -->|High| J[Cosmetic Glove Over Functional Hand]
    I -->|Low| K[Bare Hook for Maximum Function]
    G --> L[Functional Training for 6 Weeks]
    H --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> endnode[FINAL PROSTHESIS DELIVERY]
    D --> L
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Myoelectric Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[RESIDUAL MUSCLE EMG] --> node2[DIFFERENTIAL ELECTRODE PAIR]
    node2 --> node3[INSTRUMENTATION AMPLIFIER GAIN 1000]
    node3 --> node4[NOTCH FILTER 50 HZ]
    node4 --> node5[LOW PASS FILTER 500 HZ]
    node5 --> node6[RECTIFIER AND SMOOTHER]
    node6 --> node7[THRESHOLD DETECTOR]
    node7 --> node8[DIRECTION LOGIC HAND OPEN OR CLOSE]
    node8 --> node9[MOTOR DRIVER H BRIDGE]
    node9 --> node10[DC GEARMOTOR 7.4 V]
    node10 --> node11[FINGER MECHANISM]
    node11 -. Force Feedback .-> node7
    node12[LI ION BATTERY 7.4 V] --> node9
```

**KTU Mapping Note:** This figure is the standard block-level schematic asked in KTU 2024 scheme Module 4 (14-mark questions). Students must label **all five filters** and the **H-bridge direction logic** in the answer sheet.

## 4.4 Comparison Matrix — Body-Powered vs Myoelectric

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph BODY[Body Powered]
        BP1[Force Source: Shoulder]
        BP2[Transmission: Cable]
        BP3[Response: 100 ms]
        BP4[Mass: 300 to 500 g]
        BP5[Cost: 25000 to 150000 INR]
        BP6[Cosmesis: Poor to Fair]
    end
    subgraph MYO[Myoelectric]
        MY1[Force Source: Battery and Motor]
        MY2[Transmission: Gearbox and Linkage]
        MY3[Response: 300 to 400 ms]
        MY4[Mass: 400 to 700 g]
        MY5[Cost: 150000 to 600000 INR]
        MY6[Cosmesis: Excellent]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define body-powered upper-limb prosthesis. List any four advantages.**

**Model Answer:**
A body-powered upper-limb prosthesis is a mechanical prosthetic device in which the functional movements (grip, elbow flexion) are actuated by the amputee's own body movements, typically transmitted through a harness and Bowden cable.

**Four advantages:**
1. Robust and reliable, suitable for outdoor and wet environments.
2. Low cost (typically one-third of myoelectric devices).
3. Provides natural proprioceptive feedback through cable tension.
4. Faster response time (~100 ms) compared to myoelectric systems.

---

### Question 2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the function of a terminal device in an upper-limb prosthesis? Differentiate between voluntary-opening and voluntary-closing terminal devices.**

**Model Answer:**
The terminal device (TD) is the end-effector of the prosthesis that actually performs prehension (gripping and manipulation). It replaces the function of the human hand.

| Feature | Voluntary-Opening (VO) | Voluntary-Closing (VC) |
|---|---|---|
| Default State | Grip is closed by spring | Grip is open by spring |
| User Action | Opens actively | Closes actively |
| Grip Force | Limited by spring (~30–70 N) | Limited by user strength (~100–200 N) |
| Fatigue | Low (grip is free) | High (must hold) |
| Use Case | Most unilateral amputees | Bilateral amputees, heavy work |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**
**(a)** Explain the levels of upper-limb amputation with suitable diagrams and state the corresponding prosthetic considerations. (7 marks)
**(b)** Describe the construction and working of a myoelectrically controlled upper-limb prosthesis. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Definition of Levels** (2 marks)
The level of amputation is determined by the anatomical location at which the limb is severed. From distal to proximal, the KTU-recognized levels are:
1. Partial Hand
2. Wrist Disarticulation
3. Transradial (Below-Elbow)
4. Elbow Disarticulation
5. Transhumeral (Above-Elbow)
6. Shoulder Disarticulation
7. Forequarter

**Step 2 — Prosthetic Considerations by Level** (4 marks)

| Level | Functional Loss | Prosthetic Components |
|---|---|---|
| Partial Hand | Fingers/partial palm | Silicone cosmetic or mechanical finger |
| Transradial | Loss of hand + variable forearm | Socket + cable/electrodes + TD; harness optional |
| Transhumeral | Loss of entire forearm + part of arm | Same as above + prosthetic elbow joint (cable or myo) |
| Shoulder Disarticulation | Loss of entire arm | Body-powered shoulder, hybrid elbow and TD, often cosmetic shell |

**Step 3 — Design Principles** (1 mark)
The higher the amputation, the greater the number of joints to be replaced, the heavier the prosthesis, and the lower the functional outcome. Energy expenditure for above-elbow amputees is roughly **2×** that of below-elbow.

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Principle of Myoelectric Control** (2 marks)
Residual muscles (e.g., flexor digitorum superficialis, extensor carpi radialis) continue to produce electrical activity during attempted movement. Surface electrodes placed on the skin over these muscles detect the **electromyogram (EMG)** — a voltage of **20–200 µV RMS** in the **80–250 Hz** band. The signal is processed to actuate a motor-driven hand.

**Step 2 — Block Diagram of Myoelectric Hand** (3 marks)
The chain of signal processing is:

Residual Muscle → Electrodes → Instrumentation Amplifier (gain ≈ 1000) → Notch Filter (50 Hz) → Low-Pass Filter (500 Hz) → Rectifier → Threshold Detector → Motor Driver (H-Bridge) → DC Gearmotor → Finger Linkage → Grip Action.

**Step 3 — Working Description** (2 marks)
- When the user attempts to close the hand, the **flexor** muscle contracts; its EMG exceeds a threshold (typically 20 µV post-amplification = 20 mV); the controller drives the motor in the "close" direction.
- When the user attempts to open the hand, the **extensor** muscle contracts; the controller drives the motor in the "open" direction.
- A **co-contraction** (both muscles firing simultaneously) is used to switch modes (e.g., from grip to wrist rotation) in advanced hands.
- Power is supplied by a **7.4 V Li-ion battery**, providing roughly **8–12 hours** of intermittent use.

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a body-powered transradial prosthesis. (7 marks)
**(b)** Compare body-powered, externally powered, and hybrid upper-limb prosthetic systems. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Anatomy of the System** (2 marks)
A body-powered transradial (below-elbow) prosthesis consists of:
- A **figure-8 harness** worn over the contralateral shoulder.
- A **control cable** (Bowden cable, typically 1.6 mm stainless steel).
- A **triceps pad** for cable anchorage on the posterior socket.
- A **self-suspending socket** (anatomical fit over the humeral condyles) or a harness.
- A **terminal device** — usually a hook for function or a hand for cosmesis.

**Step 2 — Working Principle** (3 marks)
- When the amputee **abducts/extends** the contralateral shoulder, the harness pulls the cable.
- The cable transmits force across the elbow to the terminal device.
- The Bowden cable outer housing is fixed; only the inner cable moves.
- For elbow flexion (in transhumeral systems), a separate cable is used to lock/unlock the elbow.

**Step 3 — Force Analysis** (2 marks)
Using $MA = D_{harness}/D_{anchor}$, with typical values 100 mm and 25 mm, the mechanical advantage is **4:1**. A 12.5 N shoulder force thus produces a 50 N grip.

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks

| Parameter | Body-Powered | Externally Powered (Myoelectric) | Hybrid |
|---|---|---|---|
| Power Source | Body (shoulder) | Battery + EMG signal | Both |
| Force Transmission | Bowden cable | Motor + gearbox | Cable for elbow, motor for hand |
| Response Time | ~100 ms | ~300 ms | ~150 ms |
| Grip Force | 30–70 N (VO), 100+ N (VC) | 60–100 N | 80+ N |
| Cosmesis | Poor | Excellent | Good |
| Cost (₹) | 25,000 – 1,50,000 | 1,50,000 – 6,00,000 | 3,00,000 – 10,00,000 |
| Maintenance | Minimal (cable replacement) | High (electronics, battery) | Moderate |
| Best Suited For | Heavy labor, rural, unilateral BE | Cosmetic priority, transradial | Transhumeral, mixed use |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Confusing VO and VC:** Many students write "voluntary closing means the device closes automatically." WRONG. In VC, the user must actively close the device. Always state which is the **default** state.
> 2. **Missing the Notch Filter:** When drawing the myoelectric block diagram, students often omit the **50 Hz notch filter**. KTU expects this explicitly because it is a unique Indian/Asian requirement.
> 3. **Confusing MA formula direction:** Some students invert the cable MA formula. Remember: $MA = D_{harness} / D_{anchor}$ — the **larger** distance is at the input (shoulder).
> 4. **Omitting safety factor:** The KTU answer key requires the **1.5×** safety factor for suspension design. Writing only the dynamic weight costs one mark.
> 5. **Writing "amputation level" but not naming all 7 levels:** KTU expects all standard levels for a 7-mark question. Partial lists lose at least 2 marks.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Upper Limb Prosthetics (KTU Module 4)**

- [ ] **Definition:** Upper-limb prosthetics replaces anatomy, biomechanics, and cosmesis of the missing upper extremity.
- [ ] **7 Amputation Levels:** Partial Hand → Wrist Disarticulation → Transradial → Elbow Disarticulation → Transhumeral → Shoulder Disarticulation → Forequarter.
- [ ] **5 Subsystems:** Socket, Suspension, Control, Actuation, Terminal Device.
- [ ] **3 Control Philosophies:** Body-powered, Externally powered (myoelectric), Hybrid.
- [ ] **Cable MA formula:** $MA = D_{harness} / D_{anchor}$, typically 2:1 to 5:1.
- [ ] **EMG signal:** **20–200 µV** amplitude, **80–250 Hz** usable band, notch filter at **50 Hz**.
- [ ] **Skin pressure limit:** **40 kPa** over bony prominences.
- [ ] **VO vs VC:** VO = spring closes (free grip); VC = user closes (active, stronger, tiring).
- [ ] **Myoelectric response:** **300–400 ms** slower than body-powered (100 ms).
- [ ] **Battery:** **7.4 V Li-ion**, ~8–12 hours use.
- [ ] **Trilogy of Design:** Function, Cosmesis, Comfort — explicit trade-offs.
- [ ] **Safety factor for suspension:** **1.5×** dynamic weight.
- [ ] **Pin-lock, suction, harness:** three main suspension mechanisms.
- [ ] **Myoelectric block diagram:** Electrode → IA → Notch → LPF → Rectifier → Threshold → H-Bridge → Motor.
- [ ] **Cost ratio (Body : Myo : Hybrid):** roughly **1 : 4 : 8** in India.
- [ ] **Functional Outcome Score:** 0 (worst) – 4 (best); falls as amputation level rises proximally.
- [ ] **Mnemonic "2-2-2 Rule":** Body-powered is 2/3 the cost, 2/3 the weight, 2/3 the grip force of myoelectric — but 2/3 the cosmesis.
